content.text: Distributed Data Processing at Scale with Apache Spark and PySpark

Modern data engineering routinely involves datasets too large to fit in a single machine's memory — terabytes of logs, petabytes of sensor readings, billions of transactions. Apache Spark solves this by distributing both storage and computation across clusters of machines, processing data in parallel while presenting a unified, high-level programming interface. PySpark is the official Python API for Spark, giving Python developers full access to Spark's engine without leaving their language of choice.

is an open-source, unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing. It was created at UC Berkeley's AMPLab in 2009 and donated to the Apache Software Foundation in 2013. Spark generalises the MapReduce paradigm from Hadoop by supporting iterative algorithms, interactive queries, streaming, and machine learning — all within a single framework.
is the Python binding for Spark. It communicates with the Spark JVM runtime via

, a bridge library that lets Python objects call Java/Scala objects transparently. When a PySpark program runs, the Python process acts as the driver, while the actual distributed execution happens inside JVM worker processes on each node.
Spark's central abstraction has evolved in three generations:
Resilient Distributed Dataset — low-level, immutable partitioned collection
Structured table with named columns; uses Catalyst optimizer
Type-safe DataFrame (Scala/Java only; in Python it's always a DataFrame)
is the primary interface for most data engineering work. RDDs are still available for cases requiring fine-grained control.

Before Spark, distributed data processing meant writing verbose MapReduce jobs in Java, managing intermediate disk I/O between every map and reduce step, and running separate tools for streaming (Apache Storm) or machine learning (Mahout). Spark unified all of this:
eliminates repeated disk writes between pipeline stages, making iterative algorithms (like k-means or gradient descent) 10–100× faster than Hadoop MapReduce.
covers batch ETL, SQL analytics, streaming, graph processing, and ML — one cluster, one API.
means transformations build up a logical plan that Spark optimises holistically before execution.
through lineage: if a partition is lost, Spark recomputes it from the original source rather than maintaining expensive data replicas.
A Spark application always has exactly one

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Driver Process                      │
│  SparkSession → Logical Plan → Physical Plan          │
│  Task Scheduling → DAGScheduler → TaskScheduler       │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
               │  submits tasks via Cluster Manager
     ┌─────────▼──────────────────────────────┐
     │         Cluster Manager                 │
     │  (YARN / Kubernetes / Mesos / Standalone│
     └────┬──────────┬──────────┬─────────────┘
          │          │          │
   ┌──────▼──┐ ┌─────▼──┐ ┌────▼───┐
   │Executor │ │Executor│ │Executor│
   │ Node 1  │ │ Node 2 │ │ Node 3 │
   │ [Task]  │ │ [Task] │ │ [Task] │
   │ [Cache] │ │ [Cache]│ │ [Cache]│
   └─────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘
— Runs the user's main program. Converts user code into a DAG of stages, negotiates resources from the cluster manager, and sends tasks to executors.

— Resource allocator. Standalone mode is built into Spark. Production deployments use YARN (Hadoop clusters), Kubernetes (containerised environments), or cloud-managed services (Databricks, EMR, Dataproc).
— JVM process on a worker node. Runs the actual task code, stores cached data in memory or disk, and reports results back to the driver.
— The smallest unit of work: processing one partition of data with one function.
An RDD is an immutable, partitioned collection distributed across executor nodes. Each partition is processed by one task on one executor. RDDs support two types of operations:
— force Spark to execute the DAG.

from pyspark import SparkContext

sc = SparkContext("local[*]", "RDD Example")

data = sc.parallelize(range(1, 1_000_001))          # create RDD with 1M numbers
result = (data
          .filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0)             # keep evens (transformation)
          .map(lambda x: x * x)                     # square them (transformation)
          .reduce(lambda a, b: a + b))               # sum (action → triggers execution)

print(result)
sc.stop()
is called. Spark then optimises and executes the chain.
DataFrames add schema information on top of RDDs. This lets Spark's
query optimiser reason about the data and generate efficient physical execution plans — similar to how a SQL database engine optimises queries.

User Code (Python/SQL)
        ↓
  Unresolved Logical Plan   ← parser / DataFrame API
        ↓
  Resolved Logical Plan     ← Catalog (type checking, column resolution)
        ↓
  Optimised Logical Plan    ← Catalyst rules (predicate pushdown, projection pruning,
        ↓                      constant folding, join reordering)
  Physical Plans            ← multiple candidate physical plans
        ↓
  Selected Physical Plan    ← Cost model picks best
        ↓
  Code Generation (Tungsten) ← bytecode generated for tight JVM loops
        ↓
  Execution on Cluster
: If a filter exists on a column, Catalyst pushes that filter as close to the data source as possible (e.g., into a Parquet file reader or a JDBC query) so fewer rows are read.
: Only columns actually used are read from disk — critical for columnar formats like Parquet.
Tungsten, introduced in Spark 1.5, operates below the DataFrame API and optimises JVM execution:
lay out data to maximise CPU cache utilisation.
compiles an entire pipeline stage into a single tight Java function rather than calling virtual methods per row.
of computation stages. A stage boundary is created wherever a
is required — a shuffle being the redistribution of data across partitions based on a key (needed for groupBy, join, sort).

Stage 1: read → filter → map         (narrow transformations — no shuffle)
              ↓ shuffle
Stage 2: groupByKey → aggregate       (wide transformation — data moves between executors)
              ↓ shuffle
Stage 3: join with another dataset    (another wide transformation)
              ↓
Stage 4: write output
) process each partition independently with no data movement. Wide transformations (e.g.,
) require a shuffle — the most expensive operation in Spark because it involves network I/O and disk writes.
Every PySpark program begins with a
. It is the unified entry point introduced in Spark 2.0 that combines

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = (SparkSession
         .builder
         .appName("MyPipeline")
         .master("local[4]")                          # local mode: 4 CPU cores
         .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "200")
         .config("spark.executor.memory", "4g")
         .getOrCreate())

# Access the underlying SparkContext if needed
sc = spark.sparkContext
is not set in code — the cluster manager provides it through

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("demo").master("local[*]").getOrCreate()

# --- From a list of dicts ---
data = [
    {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "salary": 90000.0},
    {"name": "Bob",   "age": 25, "salary": 72000.0},
    {"name": "Carol", "age": 35, "salary": 115000.0},
]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data)
df.show()
df.printSchema()

# --- From CSV ---
df_csv = (spark.read
               .option("header", "true")
               .option("inferSchema", "true")
               .csv("s3://bucket/data/employees.csv"))

# --- From Parquet (preferred format for production) ---
df_parquet = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/data/transactions/")

# --- With explicit schema (safer than inferSchema for production) ---
schema = StructType([
    StructField("user_id",    StringType(),  nullable=False),
    StructField("event_type", StringType(),  nullable=True),
    StructField("amount",     DoubleType(),  nullable=True),
    StructField("ts",         IntegerType(), nullable=True),
])
df_typed = spark.read.schema(schema).json("s3://bucket/events/")
Always define an explicit schema for production pipelines.
scans the data once to guess types — slow and unreliable with messy data.

from pyspark.sql import functions as F
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# --- Filter and Select ---
active_users = (df
    .filter(F.col("status") == "active")
    .select("user_id", "email", "signup_date"))

# --- Column Expressions ---
enriched = df.withColumn(
    "age_group",
    F.when(F.col("age") < 25, "junior")
     .when(F.col("age") < 40, "mid")
     .otherwise("senior")
)

# --- String / Date Functions ---
cleaned = df.withColumn("email_lower", F.lower(F.trim(F.col("email"))))
dated = df.withColumn("signup_year", F.year(F.to_date(F.col("signup_date"), "yyyy-MM-dd")))

# --- Aggregations ---
dept_stats = (df
    .groupBy("department")
    .agg(
        F.count("*").alias("headcount"),
        F.avg("salary").alias("avg_salary"),
        F.max("salary").alias("max_salary"),
        F.percentile_approx("salary", 0.5).alias("median_salary")
    ))

# --- Window Functions ---
window_spec = Window.partitionBy("department").orderBy(F.desc("salary"))

ranked = df.withColumn("rank_in_dept", F.rank().over(window_spec))

top_earners = ranked.filter(F.col("rank_in_dept") == 1)

# --- Joins ---
employees = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/employees/")
departments = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/departments/")

joined = employees.join(departments, on="dept_id", how="left")

# Broadcast join — use when one table is small enough to fit in memory per executor
joined_broadcast = employees.join(
    F.broadcast(departments), on="dept_id", how="inner"
)

df.createOrReplaceTempView("employees")
departments.createOrReplaceTempView("departments")

result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        d.dept_name,
        COUNT(e.employee_id)   AS headcount,
        ROUND(AVG(e.salary), 2) AS avg_salary
    FROM employees e
    JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
    WHERE e.status = 'active'
    GROUP BY d.dept_name
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

result.show()
SQL and the DataFrame API produce identical execution plans — they are interchangeable.
Parquet is the de facto standard for Spark analytics. Its columnar layout lets Spark read only the columns needed (projection pruning), and its block compression (Snappy, Zstd) reduces I/O.

# Write partitioned Parquet — critical for query performance
(df
 .write
 .mode("overwrite")                               # overwrite | append | ignore | errorIfExists
 .partitionBy("year", "month")                    # creates directory hierarchy year=2024/month=01/
 .option("compression", "snappy")
 .parquet("s3://bucket/output/events/"))

# Read only specific partitions (partition pruning)
df_jan = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/output/events/year=2024/month=01/")

# Or use filter — Catalyst applies partition pruning automatically
df_filtered = (spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/output/events/")
               .filter((F.col("year") == 2024) & (F.col("month") == 1)))
Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time travel, and upserts to data lakes.

# pip install delta-spark
# SparkSession must be configured with Delta extensions

spark = (SparkSession.builder
         .config("spark.jars.packages", "io.delta:delta-core_2.12:2.4.0")
         .config("spark.sql.extensions", "io.delta.sql.DeltaSparkSessionExtension")
         .config("spark.sql.catalog.spark_catalog", "org.apache.spark.sql.delta.catalog.DeltaCatalog")
         .getOrCreate())

# Write Delta table
df.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save("s3://bucket/delta/employees")

# Upsert (merge) — ACID upsert, impossible with plain Parquet
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, "s3://bucket/delta/employees")

(delta_table.alias("target")
 .merge(
     updates_df.alias("source"),
     "target.employee_id = source.employee_id"
 )
 .whenMatchedUpdateAll()
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll()
 .execute())

# Time travel
df_yesterday = spark.read.format("delta").option("versionAsOf", 5).load("s3://bucket/delta/employees")
Spark Structured Streaming treats a live data stream as an unbounded table that grows over time. The same DataFrame API applies.

# Read from Kafka
stream_df = (spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "broker1:9092,broker2:9092")
    .option("subscribe", "user_events")
    .option("startingOffsets", "latest")
    .load())

# Kafka messages arrive as binary — parse them
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, LongType
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

event_schema = StructType([
    StructField("user_id",     StringType(), True),
    StructField("event_type",  StringType(), True),
    StructField("amount",      DoubleType(), True),
    StructField("event_time",  LongType(),   True),
])

parsed = (stream_df
    .selectExpr("CAST(value AS STRING) as json_str")
    .select(F.from_json(F.col("json_str"), event_schema).alias("data"))
    .select("data.*")
    .withColumn("event_ts", F.to_timestamp(F.col("event_time") / 1000)))

# Windowed aggregation with watermark (handles late data)
windowed = (parsed
    .withWatermark("event_ts", "10 minutes")      # tolerate data up to 10 min late
    .groupBy(
        F.window(F.col("event_ts"), "5 minutes"), # 5-minute tumbling window
        F.col("event_type")
    )
    .agg(F.sum("amount").alias("total_amount"),
         F.count("*").alias("event_count")))

# Write to Parquet sink
query = (windowed.writeStream
    .outputMode("append")              # append | update | complete
    .format("parquet")
    .option("path", "s3://bucket/streaming/output/")
    .option("checkpointLocation", "s3://bucket/streaming/checkpoints/")
    .trigger(processingTime="1 minute")
    .start())

query.awaitTermination()
are crucial: they tell Spark how late data can arrive and still be included in a window, after which Spark can safely discard state for that window.
Spark MLlib provides scalable ML algorithms that operate on DataFrames (the newer

from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
from pyspark.ml.feature import VectorAssembler, StandardScaler, StringIndexer
from pyspark.ml.classification import LogisticRegression, RandomForestClassifier
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import BinaryClassificationEvaluator
from pyspark.ml.tuning import CrossValidator, ParamGridBuilder

# Assume df has columns: age, salary, department (string), churn (0/1)

# Stage 1: encode categorical
indexer = StringIndexer(inputCol="department", outputCol="dept_index")

# Stage 2: assemble features into a single vector column
assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=["age", "salary", "dept_index"],
    outputCol="features_raw"
)

# Stage 3: scale features
scaler = StandardScaler(inputCol="features_raw", outputCol="features")

# Stage 4: classifier
rf = RandomForestClassifier(featuresCol="features", labelCol="churn", numTrees=100)

# Build pipeline
pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[indexer, assembler, scaler, rf])

# Train / test split
train, test = df.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

# Hyperparameter tuning
param_grid = (ParamGridBuilder()
    .addGrid(rf.numTrees, [50, 100, 200])
    .addGrid(rf.maxDepth, [5, 10])
    .build())

evaluator = BinaryClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="churn", metricName="areaUnderROC")

cv = CrossValidator(
    estimator=pipeline,
    estimatorParamMaps=param_grid,
    evaluator=evaluator,
    numFolds=3,
    parallelism=4    # evaluate 4 param combos simultaneously
)

cv_model = cv.fit(train)

# Evaluate
predictions = cv_model.transform(test)
auc = evaluator.evaluate(predictions)
print(f"AUC on test set: {auc:.4f}")

# Save model
cv_model.bestModel.save("s3://bucket/models/churn_model_v1")

When built-in Spark functions cannot express the required logic, UDFs allow arbitrary Python code. However, UDFs are expensive because Spark must serialise each row from JVM memory, send it to the Python process, run the Python function, and serialise the result back.
for zero-copy batch serialisation between JVM and Python, making them typically 5–100× faster than row-level UDFs.
Architecture of a Production Data Pipeline
A realistic ETL pipeline reading from multiple sources, transforming, and writing to a data lake:

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, functions as F
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType

def build_spark_session():
    return (SparkSession.builder
            .appName("sales-etl-pipeline")
            .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")
            .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")        # AQE: auto-tune at runtime
            .config("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
            .config("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")
            .getOrCreate())


def read_raw_transactions(spark, path: str):
    schema = StructType([
        StructField("transaction_id", StringType(),   False),
        StructField("user_id",        StringType(),   True),
        StructField("product_id",     StringType(),   True),
        StructField("amount",         DoubleType(),   True),
        StructField("currency",       StringType(),   True),
        StructField("ts",             TimestampType(),True),
        StructField("status",         StringType(),   True),
    ])
    return spark.read.schema(schema).parquet(path)


def clean_transactions(df):
    return (df
        .filter(F.col("status") == "completed")
        .filter(F.col("amount") > 0)
        .filter(F.col("user_id").isNotNull())
        .dropDuplicates(["transaction_id"])
        .withColumn("amount_usd",
                    F.when(F.col("currency") == "EUR", F.col("amount") * 1.08)
                     .when(F.col("currency") == "GBP", F.col("amount") * 1.27)
                     .otherwise(F.col("amount")))
        .withColumn("txn_date",    F.to_date(F.col("ts")))
        .withColumn("txn_year",    F.year(F.col("ts")))
        .withColumn("txn_month",   F.month(F.col("ts"))))


def compute_user_metrics(df):
    return (df
        .groupBy("user_id", "txn_year", "txn_month")
        .agg(
            F.sum("amount_usd").alias("monthly_spend"),
            F.count("*").alias("txn_count"),
            F.max("ts").alias("last_purchase_ts"),
            F.countDistinct("product_id").alias("unique_products")
        ))


def write_output(df, output_path: str):
    (df.write
       .mode("overwrite")
       .partitionBy("txn_year", "txn_month")
       .option("compression", "zstd")
       .parquet(output_path))


def main():
    spark = build_spark_session()

    raw = read_raw_transactions(spark, "s3://data-lake/raw/transactions/")
    products = spark.read.parquet("s3://data-lake/reference/products/")

    cleaned   = clean_transactions(raw)
    with_meta = cleaned.join(F.broadcast(products), on="product_id", how="left")
    metrics   = compute_user_metrics(with_meta)

    metrics.cache()   # materialise — used multiple times below
    write_output(metrics, "s3://data-lake/curated/user_monthly_metrics/")

    summary = (metrics
               .groupBy("txn_year", "txn_month")
               .agg(F.sum("monthly_spend").alias("total_revenue"),
                    F.countDistinct("user_id").alias("active_users")))
    write_output(summary, "s3://data-lake/curated/monthly_summary/")

    spark.stop()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Submit to YARN cluster
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --num-executors 20 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 8g \
  --driver-memory 4g \
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=400 \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true \
  --py-files dependencies.zip \
  pipeline.py

# Submit to Kubernetes
spark-submit \
  --master k8s://https://<k8s-api-server>:6443 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=my-registry/spark-pyspark:3.5 \
  --conf spark.executor.instances=10 \
  pipeline.py
Partitioning is the most impactful tuning lever. Each partition becomes one task on one CPU core.
: cores sit idle, memory pressure on each task.
: scheduling overhead dominates, tiny files on output.
) is wrong for most production workloads. A common heuristic: target 128–512 MB of data per partition.

# Explicit repartition (causes a shuffle — avoid unless necessary)
df_repartitioned = df.repartition(400, "user_id")  # hash-partition by user_id

# Coalesce — reduce partition count WITHOUT a full shuffle (merge local partitions)
df_coalesced = df.coalesce(50)  # use after filter that removed most data
AQE, enabled by default in Spark 3.2+, dynamically adjusts the query plan at runtime based on actual data statistics collected during execution:
: detects skewed partitions and splits them.
: can convert a sort-merge join to a broadcast join if one side turns out to be small.

from pyspark import StorageLevel

# Default: MEMORY_AND_DISK — spills to disk if memory is insufficient
df.cache()

# Fine-grained storage level control
df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)          # pure memory, fastest, evicts if OOM
df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER)  # serialised (less RAM), spills to disk
df.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)            # disk only, slowest, good for very large sets

# Always unpersist when done to free executor memory
df.unpersist()
Cache DataFrames that are used more than once in the same application. Do not cache DataFrames used only once — it wastes memory.

Skew occurs when one partition contains vastly more data than others (e.g., a join key where one value appears millions of times). Symptoms: one task runs 100× longer than siblings, job hangs near 99% completion.

# Option 1: Salt the skewed key to distribute load
import random

SALT_FACTOR = 50

skewed_df = skewed_df.withColumn(
    "salted_key",
    F.concat(F.col("skewed_col"), F.lit("_"), (F.rand() * SALT_FACTOR).cast("int"))
)

right_df_exploded = right_df.withColumn(
    "salted_key",
    F.explode(F.array([F.concat(F.col("key"), F.lit(f"_{i}")) for i in range(SALT_FACTOR)]))
)

result = skewed_df.join(right_df_exploded, on="salted_key")

# Option 2: Enable AQE skew join handling (Spark 3.0+)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor", "5")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes", "256m")
pulls all data to the driver. On a 10-billion-row DataFrame this means OOM on the driver node. Use
Using Python UDFs instead of built-in functions
— Built-in Spark SQL functions execute natively in the JVM. Python UDFs incur a serialisation penalty per row. Always check the
scans the entire dataset to guess types. This doubles read time and is unreliable with nulls or mixed types.
at 200 for a 10 TB dataset means each shuffle partition is 50 GB — will cause OOM. Tune this based on data volume.
in the middle of a pipeline for debugging
— Every action triggers a full DAG execution. Using many intermediate actions in a pipeline multiplies total execution time. Use the Spark UI or sampling (
Not caching DataFrames reused multiple times
— Without caching, Spark recomputes the full lineage for every action. If a DataFrame is used in two branches of a pipeline, cache it after it is built.
shuffles all values to the reducer.
applies the combiner locally before shuffling, massively reducing network I/O. On DataFrames, prefer
Prefer the DataFrame/SQL API over raw RDDs.
Catalyst and Tungsten optimisations apply only to DataFrames. RDDs bypass these and execute as-is.
Partition output data by the most common query predicates.
, partition by those columns so Spark reads only relevant directories.
Use Parquet with Snappy or Zstd compression
for all intermediate and curated data. Snappy is fast with moderate compression. Zstd compresses better at similar speed — preferred for cold storage.
The default broadcast threshold is 10 MB (
). Raise it for tables up to a few hundred MB that fit in executor memory.
before writes to control output file count.
Writing 1000 partitions produces 1000 small files which is slow for future readers. Coalesce or repartition to produce 128–512 MB files.
on Spark 3.x — it handles many tuning decisions automatically at runtime.
Always provide a checkpoint location for streaming jobs.
Without it, the job cannot recover from failure and will reprocess data from the beginning.
— read functions, transform functions, write functions. Makes unit testing with
Alternative open table format with schema evolution
Event streaming input for Structured Streaming
Managed Spark platform with Unity Catalog, Delta Live Tables
Built-in web UI (port 4040) for job/stage/task monitoring
PySpark DataFrame testing utility (assert equality, schema checks)
the data volume exceeds a single machine's memory, batch ETL pipelines need SQL-grade optimisation, or the workload combines batch + streaming + ML in one platform.
the dataset fits comfortably in RAM and single-machine speed is sufficient — Pandas is faster for small data due to zero distribution overhead.
true sub-second latency streaming with stateful event-time processing is required — Spark Structured Streaming's micro-batch model introduces inherent latency.
) and at rest (handled at the storage layer: S3 SSE, HDFS encryption zones). Kerberos authentication integrates with YARN for cluster security.
— Use Databricks Unity Catalog or Apache Atlas to track data lineage, enforce column-level access control, and audit data access.

— Spark's lineage-based fault tolerance handles executor failures automatically by recomputing lost partitions. For streaming jobs, checkpointing persists offsets and state to durable storage so the job resumes from the last committed offset after a driver failure.
— Write to a staging path, validate, then atomically rename/move to the final path. Or use Delta Lake's ACID transactions to guarantee exactly-once write semantics.
— In multi-tenant clusters, use dynamic resource allocation and YARN queues or Kubernetes namespaces to prevent one job from starving others.
— Use Delta Lake or Iceberg, which support adding columns without rewriting existing data. With plain Parquet, schema changes require full dataset rewrites.
Spark distributes computation by splitting data into partitions processed in parallel across executor JVMs; PySpark is the Python driver interface to this system.
The DataFrame API with Catalyst optimisation is far more efficient than raw RDDs and should be the default choice in all modern PySpark code.
Shuffles — caused by wide transformations like
— are the primary performance bottleneck; minimise their number and size.
Adaptive Query Execution (Spark 3.2+) handles many tuning decisions automatically, but explicit schema definitions, correct partitioning, and broadcast joins remain essential manual optimisations.
Pandas UDFs with Apache Arrow serialisation are the only acceptable form of Python UDF for performance-critical code; row-level Python UDFs should be avoided.
Delta Lake adds ACID transactions, upserts, and time travel on top of Parquet, making it the production-grade storage layer for data lakes built on Spark.
Structured Streaming applies the same DataFrame API to unbounded data streams, with watermarks handling late data and checkpointing providing fault tolerance.
